2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.1 Module 1 - Codebase Analysis
1. Project Structure Extraction
1.1.1 The system shall scan the active project folder and subfolders to identify all
.py files.
1.1.2 The system shall only process files with the .py extension for analysis and
content extraction.
1.1.3 The system shall structure the extracted content in a format that allows for
further analysis.
2. Abstract Syntax Tree Generation
22
2.2 Functional Requirements
1.2.1 The system shall generate an AST for each Python file.
1.2.2 The system shall automatically re-generate the AST for a file whenever project
is saved.
1.2.3 The system shall indicate whenever AST generation for a specific file fails.
1.2.4 The system shall restructure the AST to ensure further processing.
3. Code Dependency Graph Generation
1.3.1 The system shall create an inter-file dependency graph by analyzing the code-
base to identify how different files are related to each other.
1.3.3 The system shall update the inter-file dependency graph whenever the AST is
updated.
1.3.4 The system shall represent files as nodes of the dependency graph.
1.3.5 The system shall represent inter-file dependencies as directed edges in the
dependency graph.
1.3.6 The system shall ensure that the graph accurately reflects the current state of
the codebase, including all inter-file connections and dependencies.
2.2.2 Module 2 - Detection Module
1. Real-Time Detection of Code Smells
2.1.1 The system shall scan all the Python files in the workspace for code smells
after regular intervals.
2.1.2 The system shall run detection on all the scanned files for any present code
smells.
i. The system shall detect occurrences of magic numbers within the code-
base.
ii. The system shall detect unutilized variables within the codebase.
iii. The system shall detect occurrences of dead code within the codebase.
iv. The system shall detect functions or methods with long parameter lists.
v. The system shall detect conflicts caused by the use of global variables.
vi. The system shall detect overly complex conditional statements within the
codebase.
vii. The system shall detect occurrences of too many nested loops within the
codebase
viii. The system shall detect occurrences of unreachable code within the code-
base.
23
2. Project Requirements
ix. The system shall detect excessive use of flags within the codebase.
x. The system shall detect violations of variable naming conventions within
the codebase.
xi. The system should detect inconsistent naming within the codebase.
xii. The system should detect non-descriptive method names within the code-
base
2.1.3 The system shall allow the user to view identified code smells.
2. User Triggered Detection of Code Smells
2.2.1 The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of certain
code smells.
i. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of long
functions.
ii. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of switch
statement abuser.
iii. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection for tight
coupling.
iv. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection for god
object.
v. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of in-
consistent naming.
vi. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of fea-
ture envy.
vii. The system should allow the user to manually trigger the detection of
code fragments that have been commented out.
viii. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection tempo-
rary field.
ix. The system should allow the user to manually trigger the detection over-
encapsulation.
x. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection middle
man.
2.2.2 The system shall allow the user to view the problematic code snippets within
the file.
2.2.3 The system shall enable the user to view details about the issue detected in the
selected problematic code snippet.
24
2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.3 Module 3 - Refactoring Module
3.1 Refactoring Readiness Assessment of Identified Code Smells
3.1.1 The system shall evaluate the refactoring readiness of the code.
i. The system shall evaluate the complexity of each identified code smell.
ii. The system shall evaluate the dependencies, as well as the impact of
changes associated with each identified code smell.
3.1.2 The system shall use the dependency graph to determine files that could be
affected by refactoring.
3.3 Refactoring of Identified Code Smells
3.2.1 The system shall allow the user to manually refactor the code.
3.2.2 If the code passes the refactoring readiness assessment, then the system shall
allow the user to use the automatic refactoring option to fix their code.
3.2.3 The system shall allow the user to analyze the changes made through refactor-
ing.
3.3 Filter and Refactor Specific Code Based on Detected Code Smells
3.3.1 The system shall allow the user to filter out which code smells to refactor.
3.4 Cascading Code Refactoring
3.4.1 The system shall propagate any changes made during the automated refactor-
ing process.
3.4.2 The system shall ensure consistency in variable and function names as well as
any other relevant code structures across the entire codebase.
3.5 Isolated Compilation and Testing of Refactored Code Base
3.5.1 The system shall allow the user to do a test run of the refactored codebase.
3.5.2 The system shall run the refactored code base in an isolated environment.
3.5.3 The system shall allow the user to validate the functionality of the refactored
code base.
3.6 Isolated Compilation and Testing of Refactored Code Snippet (Supplemen-
tary)
3.6.1 The system shall allow the user to do a test run of the refactored code.
3.6.2 The system shall run the refactored code snippet in an isolated environment.
25
2. Project Requirements
3.6.3 The system shall display the output of both the refactored and the original
code snippets.
3.6.4 The system shall compare the outputs of both the refactored and the original
code snippets.
3.7 Personalized Refactoring Suggestions (Supplementary)
3.7.1 The system shall analyze user coding practices.
3.7.2 The system shall provide refactoring suggestions according to users coding
practices and style.
2.2.4 Module 4 - Change Logs Module
4.1 Code Smell Detection and Refactoring Tracker
4.1.1 The system shall track all the code smells identified in the detection process.
4.1.2 The system shall track all the automated refactors.
4.1.3 The system shall maintain a detailed change logs of detection and refactoring.
4.1.4 The system shall track the current status of each code smell and update it as
changes occur.
4.1.5 The system should allow users to manually update the status of a code smell.
4.1.6 The system shall continuously track the changes in inter-file dependencies
caused by refactoring.
4.1.7 The system shall allow users to generate reports of change logs.
4.2 File Dependency Tracker
4.2.1 The system shall track inter-file dependencies within the project.
4.2.2 The system shall maintain a history of the inter-file dependencies.
4.3 Revert Readiness Assessment
4.3.1 The system shall perform a revert readiness assessment before trying to revert
a refactored code snippet.
4.3.2 The system shall analyze the impact of the revert across the project.
4.3.3 If the user has manually modified the automatically refactored code snippets,
then the system shall prevent reverting code changes.
4.3.5 If no issues are detected., then the system shall allow the user to proceed with
the revert.
26
2.2 Functional Requirements
4.3.6 If a revert is not possible due to problems,then the system shall notify the user.
4.4 Code Rollback
4.4.2 The system shall replace the refactored code with the original code when re-
vert is triggered.
4.4.3 The system shall ensure that all changes are reverted consistently across the
codebase.
4.4.4 The system shall notify the user upon successful completion of revert.
2.2.5 Module 5 - Rulesets Module
5.1 VS Code Extension Rulesets
5.1.1 The system shall allow the user to filter out which code smells to detect.
5.1.2 The system shall allow the user to filter out which files to consider during
detection.
5.1.3 The system shall allow the user to filter out which code smells to refactor
automatically.
5.1.4 The system shall allow the user to filter out which files to refactor automati-
cally.
2.2.6 Module 6 - Website Development Module
6.1 The system shall allow the user to create an account for codeNexus.
6.2 The system shall allow the user to login to their account using username and pass-
word.
6.3 The system shall allow the user to link their account with the vscode extension.
6.4 Shareable Logs Access
6.4.1 The system shall allow project owners to invite viewers to their project.
6.4.2 The system shall allow project owners to view all the viewers of their project.
6.4.3 The system shall allow viewers to view project owners and other viewers of a
project.
6.4.4 The logs shall display detailed information about each identified code smell.
6.4.5 The system shall allow users to filter logs by specific code smells, file loca-
tions, and dates.
27
2. Project Requirements
6.4.6 The system shall maintain records of all refactoring actions taken on code
smells.
6.5 Project Visualization
6.5.1 The system shall allow the user to view all the files within a project.
6.5.2 The system shall allow the user to visualize the folder structure of a project.
6.5.3 The system shall allow the user to visualize the inter-file dependencies present
in a project.
6.5.4 The system shall visualize how multiple files in the codebase are related.
6.6 Inter-File Impact Analysis
6.6.1 The system shall allow the user to view file dependencies by showing how
modifications in methods, classes, or variables in one file may impact other
files in the codebase.
6.7 Code Smells Timeline
6.7.1 The system shall display a history of code smells detected over time.
6.7.2 The system shall display the number of detections made during each scan.
6.7.3 If a code smell has been refactored automatically,then the system shall display
the modified code snippet .
6.7.4 The system should utilize graphs to show how the number of code smells has
evolved in a code base over time.
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.3.1 Reliability
REL-1 The system shall log all failures and errors.
REL-2 The system shall handle unexpected errors without losing user progress or critical
information.
REL-3 Code reversion features shall restore the code to its original state without causing
any additional issues or inconsistencies.
REL-4 Real-time detection shall update as code changes occur, ensuring that the most re-
cent state of the codebase is always analyzed.
REL-5 Logs and change history shall be recorded and retrievable at any time for review.
28
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.3.2 Usability
USE-1 The visual markers and "Problems" tab interface should be easy to interpret and
navigate.
USE-2 The system shall allow customization of code smell detection and refactoring rules
for flexibility based on different project requirements.
USE-3 Real-time detection shall not disrupt the coding experience, and the results should
be displayed as the developer edits code.
2.3.3 Performance
PER 2 Log generation and updates in the database shall occur without slowing down the
active code analysis and refactoring processes.
PER 3 The LLM Service should provide suggestions and assessments in a timely manner.
2.3.4 Security
SEC-1 The system shall restrict unauthorized access to project logs.
SEC-2 The system shall automatically log out users after a one week of inactivity.
SEC-3 The extension shall allow access only to the open and active project folder and shall
not scan files or directories outside the user’s designated workspace.
SEC-4 User actions, including code smell detection and refactoring, shall be logged se-
curely to prevent tampering or data loss.
2.3.5 Scalability
SCA-1 The detection service should be able to handle large files without performance is-
sues.
SCA-2 The system shall handle large codebases with multiple files and folders.
SCA-3 The extension shall manage multiple user-triggered detection requests, maintaining
smooth functionality for both single-file and project-wide analysis.
SCA-4 The Changelogs Service shall scale to accommodate an extensive history of code
changes, maintaining performance in log retrieval and filtering.
29

Chapter 3
System Overview
3.1 Architectural Design
The architecture includes two client interfaces: a VS Code extension and a web applica-
tion, which interact with an API Gateway. The gateway is connected to a load balancer
provided by Amazon AWS, responsible for distributing incoming traffic efficiently. The
API Gateway routes requests to one of four microservices: Code Smell Detection, Refac-
toring, Refactoring Readiness, and Changelogs.
Among these microservices, the Changelogs service requires authentication for access,
while the Code Smell Detection, Refactoring, and Refactoring Readiness services do not
require authentication. However, the Detection and Refactoring microservices have the
capability to interact with the Changelogs microservice to store and retrieve data. For
these interactions, they must pass user authentication to gain access to changelog data.
The Changelogs microservice has access to a MongoDB database to store and manage
changelog information. It acts as the central hub for all data related tasks across the
system.
In addition to these microservices, there is an LLM (Large Language Model) microservice
that performs advanced processing tasks. This microservice uses Amazon SQS to manage
task queues, where Detection and Refactoring services send their tasks. Once a task is
processed by the LLM, the completed response is added to a message queue, which the
API Gateway accesses to send corresponding messages back to the appropriate client
sockets.
To aid its operations, the LLM microservice uses a VectorDB, enabling vector retrieval for
tasks requiring advanced data processing or contextual information. It also uses a cache
to store frequently accessed vectors, improving response times and reducing redundant
data fetching.
31
3. System Overview
Figure 3.1: Architecture of CodeNexus
The architecture efficiently distributes traffic through the AWS load balancer and manages
the routing and processing of various requests through a combination of authentication
checks, microservices, and an task-processing system with the LLM microservice and
Amazon SQS.

3.3 Data Design
The JSON schema defines the structure of how data is stored and managed within a VS
Code extension for Python projects. Each project has its workspace data, tracking folder
structure, file data, inter-file dependencies, and potential code smells. The following key
components are detailed in the schema:
• Project Structure:
– Each project workspace consists of three primary data objects:
* Folder Structure: This object reprsents the complete structure of the
project directory. The FolderStructure includes all files and subfold-
ers, tracking the entire hierarchy of the project’s file system.
* File Data: For each Python file (.py) in the project, information is main-
tained:
· Code and AST: The raw code of the file and its parsed Abstract
Syntax Tree (AST) are stored for analysis and code analysis.
· Functions: The list of functions defined within the file..
56
3.3 Data Design
· Classes: Details of any classes within the file, capturing variables,
methods, and calls.
· Global Variables: Information about all global variables declared in
the file.
· Imports: Data on all imports from other files, such as imported mod-
ules, classes, or functions, including their names and types. This
helps in understanding the dependencies.
* Dependency Graph: A separate object that represents the inter-file de-
pendencies across the project:
· This is a map of FileNodes, where eachFileNode contains the name
of a .py file.
· Each FileNode has a set of DependencyNodes that represent its re-
lationships with other files within the project.
· A DependencyNode keeps track of a list of UtilizedEntities,
which are details about functions, classes, or variables that are either:
· Exported to another file.
· Imported from another file.
· This structure allows tracking of how each file interacts with others
in terms of sharing and utilizing code elements.
• Code Smells:
– The schema includes an object dedicated to tracking and managing code
smells across the project. This data structure supports 10 different types of
code smells, each as an object that stores:
* Line Numbers and Description: The specific parts of the code where
the smells were identified, along with a description of the issue.
* Tracking and Analysis: For each code smell type, attributes are used
to track whether the analysis was successful, any errors that occurred,
and the specific data related to the smell.
– This data is imprtant for assessing code quality and identifying potential areas
of improvement.
• Attributes and Data Types:
– FileNode Object: A separate object that tracks each .py file’s key attributes,
including its name and its code content. It serves as a reference to single files
and helps manage their connections to other files in the project through the
dependency graph.
57
3. System Overview
– DependencyNode: A sub-object that provides the details of a file’s depen-
dency, specifying the UtilizedEntities shared between files and whether
the file imports or exports classes, functions, or variables.
– CodeSmells Object: For each code smell detected, details such asline_numbers,
unique_violations, unused_variables, and potential conflicts are tracked.
The object records success status, errors, and relevant data for detailed anal-
ysis.
• Data Relationships and Integrity:
– The FolderStructure organizes how files and subfolders are layerd within
the project.
– Each file’s File Data allows for parsing and analyzing the Python code,
which is important for features like code navigation, refactoring, and in-depth
code quality assessments.
– The Dependency Graph maintains the relationships between different .py
files, providing a map of inter-file dependencies.
– The Code Smells object is for identifying and tracking issues in the code-
base, allowing users to improve code quality and maintainability.
This schema allows the VS Code extension to give a detailed view of the project’s struc-
ture, code details, dependencies, and code quality, thereby aiding developers in analyzing,
and improving their Python projects.
58

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
teams.
1.2 Scope
The project aims to create an intelligent software solution that automates the generation
of user stories from Software Requirements Specification (SRS) documents. By accept-
ing an SRS document and a user prompt as inputs, the software will generate contex-
tually accurate user stories. Key functionalities include parsing and analyzing the SRS,
maintaining context throughout the process, ensuring alignment with the specified re-
quirements and self evaluating the user stories. The software will allow customization
through prompts and will incorporate self-evaluation mechanisms to ensure accurate and
relevant user story generation. Additionally, the tool will maintain a history of interac-
tions for consistent output across sessions. The scope of the project includes developing
a user-friendly interface and a backend engine consisting of fine tuned LLMs working in
harmony.
1.3 Modules
1.3.1 User Input Module
This module allows users (e.g., Product Managers, Business Analysts) to upload an SRS
document along with prompts. It provides a user-friendly interface where users can
specify what kind of user stories they want to generate, whether it’s for functional, non-
functional, or specific sections of the provided SRS.
Key Features:
1. SRS document upload
2. Prompt field for user story specification
3. Input validation for ensuring correct file format and prompt structure
1.3.2 Natural Language Processing (NLP) Engine
The NLP engine processes the uploaded SRS document and interprets the user’s provided
prompt. It extracts key elements such as requirements, actors, and system behaviors from
the SRS and translates them into user stories. The engine ensures that technical language
2
1.3 Modules
is transformed into user-friendly language suitable for user stories.
Key Features:
1. Requirement extraction
2. Contextual understanding of the SRS document
3. Prompt-driven interpretation for tailored user stories
1.3.3 User Story Generation Module
This is the core module responsible for transforming the extracted data from the NLP en-
gine into coherent user stories. It adheres to a predefined format (e.g., “As a [role], I want
[goal], so that [benefit]”). The module ensures that both functional and non-functional
requirements are covered, based on the input provided.
Key Features:
1. Auto-generation of user stories
2. Handling functional and non-functional requirements
3. Customization based on the user’s prompt
1.3.4 Story Review and Editing Module
After generating user stories, this module allows users (e.g.,Product Managers, Business
Analysts, Developers etc.) to review and edit the output. It provides an interface where
users can modify the generated stories, add acceptance criteria, and provide feedback on
any necessary changes or introduce a perspective for context.
Key Features:
1. User story editing
2. Acceptance criteria addition
3. Feedback mechanisms for adjusting story generation rules
3
1. Introduction
1.3.5 V ersion Control and Tracking Module
This module tracks changes made to user stories, allowing users to maintain different
versions. It ensures that the history of changes is available for future reference, aiding
collaboration among team members.
Key Features:
1. Version history tracking
2. Story comparison between versions
3. Collaboration support with multiple users
1.3.6 Security and Authentication Module
This module handles user authentication and security, ensuring that only authorized per-
sonnel can access the system. It includes user login, password management, and session
control features.
Key Features:
1. User authentication and login
2. Role-based access control
3. Session management and encryption
4
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
User class Description
Product Manager A Product Manager is responsible for overseeing product develop-
ment. They will use the system to input an SRS document and
prompt for generating user stories. Product Managers require de-
tailed and accurate user stories that are aligned with business and
technical requirements.
Software Developer Software Developers use the generated user stories to implement fea-
tures and functionality. Developers rely on clear, technical user sto-
ries with well-defined acceptance criteria and may need the ability to
request revisions or more detailed descriptions.
Business Analyst Business Analysts bridge the gap between stakeholders and the de-
velopment team. Analysts refine the SRS before submission and use
the system to ensure user stories align with business goals. They may
work collaboratively to edit or expand stories as necessary.
QA Tester QA Testers use the user stories to create test cases and scenarios. A
QA Testers primary interaction with the system is to ensure stories
have clearly defined acceptance criteria and are testable.
Stakeholders Stakeholders review the output stories at a high level, ensuring they
align with the project’s vision. Their interaction is primarily for feed-
back and approval, as they may not be involved in the detailed cre-
ation or technical aspects of the stories.
Project Supervisor Project Supervisors monitor overall progress and ensure that user sto-
ries align with defined goals. They check stories for consistency with
the project scope and may use the system to generate reports or sum-
maries based on the stories.
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
The table 1.41.4 above outlines the various user classes anticipated to interact with the sys-
tem that generates user stories from SRS documents. Each class is defined based on its
role in the product development lifecycle, detailing their interactions with the system and
the specific requirements they have from the generated user stories (Product managers,
Software developers ,etc). Each class has distinct needs, ranging from high-level over-
sight to technical implementation. This classification ensures that the system caters to all
user roles effectively, aligning the output with both business and technical objectives.

2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.1 User Input
1. The module shall provide functionality to allow users to upload SRS (Software Re-
quirements Specification) documents.
Details:
• The module should support document formats such as .doc, .docx, and .pdf.
• Users should be able to browse their local file system and select the SRS doc-
ument to upload.
• The system must notify users upon successful upload or in case of failure with
appropriate error messages.
2. The module shall provide a prompt input field where users can specify the type of
user stories they want to generate.
Details:
• The prompt field should support text input, allowing users to specify require-
ments for functional, non-functional, or specific sections of the SRS.
• The interface should guide the users with examples or suggestions on how to
write effective prompts.
22
2. Project Requirements
• The system must process and store the prompt for subsequent user story gen-
eration.
3. The module shall validate the uploaded SRS document and the prompt input to en-
sure they meet the required format and structure.
Details:
• The system must check the file type of the uploaded document and reject
unsupported formats with an appropriate error message.
• The prompt field must have validation rules to ensure the prompt meets the
minimum and maximum length requirements.
• The system should provide real-time feedback to users if the uploaded docu-
ment or entered prompt does not meet the required criteria.
• The validation process must be efficient to ensure a smooth user experience.
2.2.2 Natural Language Processing (NLP) Engine
1. The NLP engine shall extract key requirements from the uploaded SRS document.
Details:
• The engine should identify and extract functional and non-functional require-
ments from the SRS document.
• It should recognize and tag different components such as goals, constraints,
and assumptions.
• The engine must handle diverse document structures and terminologies to ac-
curately capture the requirements.
2. The NLP engine shall understand the context of the SRS document to accurately
interpret its content.
Details:
• The engine must be capable of handling complex language constructs and
technical jargon to derive meaningful insights.
3. The NLP engine shall interpret the user’s prompt to generate tailored user stories.
Details:
23
2.2 Functional Requirements
• The engine should process the user’s prompt to understand specific require-
ments for user stories (e.g., focus on particular sections, functional vs. non-
functional).
• It must translate technical language and detailed requirements into clear, user-
friendly language suitable for user stories.
• The generated user stories should be coherent, contextually accurate, and
aligned with the user’s prompt specifications.
• The engine should provide feedback to users if the prompt is ambiguous or
insufficient for generating meaningful user stories.
2.2.3 User Story Generation
1. The module shall automatically generate user stories from the data extracted by the
NLP engine.
Details:
• The system should adhere to a predefined format for user stories, such as “As
a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]”.
• The module must ensure that each user story is coherent, concise, and follows
the standard user story structure.
• The generation process should be efficient, providing quick results to the user.
2. The module shall be capable of generating user stories for both functional and non-
functional requirements.
Details:
• The system should distinguish between functional and non-functional require-
ments and generate appropriate user stories for each.
• Functional requirements user stories should focus on describing the actions
and features of the system.
• Non-functional requirements user stories should address performance, secu-
rity, usability, and other quality attributes.
• The module must ensure that all relevant requirements extracted by the NLP
engine are covered in the generated user stories.
3. The module shall allow customization of user stories based on the user’s prompt.
Details:
24
2. Project Requirements
• The module should interpret the user’s prompt to tailor the user stories accord-
ing to the specific instructions provided.
• It should allow customization in terms of focus areas, level of detail, and spe-
cific sections or types of requirements.
• The system must ensure that the generated user stories align with the user’s
prompts while maintaining coherence and clarity.
2.2.4 Story Review and Editing
1. The module shall allow users to review and edit the generated user stories.
Details:
• Users should be able to modify the text of the user stories to better fit their
needs or correct any inaccuracies.
• The interface should provide a user-friendly text editor with basic formatting
options (e.g., bold, italic, underline).
• Changes made by users should be saved and reflected in the final user story
output.
• The system must provide version control to track changes and allow users to
revert to previous versions if needed.
2. The module shall provide mechanisms for users to give feedback on the generated
user stories and suggest adjustments to the generation rules.
Details:
• Users should be able to submit feedback on specific user stories, including
suggestions for improvements or noting any issues.
• The system should collect and analyze feedback to identify common patterns
or areas for improvement in the story generation process.
• The interface should allow users to propose new rules or modifications to ex-
isting rules for generating user stories.
• The module should facilitate communication between users and developers to
ensure feedback is addressed and incorporated into future updates.
25
2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.5 V ersion Control and Tracking
1. The module shall provide functionality to track all changes made to user stories,
capturing each version as a separate entity.
Details:
• The system should create a new version of a user story every time it is edited
and saved.
• Metadata for each version, including the author of the changes, timestamp,
and a brief description of the changes, must be stored.
• A version history view, listing all versions of a user story in chronological
order, should be available.
• Users should have the ability to revert to any previous version of a user story.
2. The module shall provide functionality for users to compare different versions of a
user story to identify changes.
Details:
• A comparison tool should be available to highlight differences between two
selected versions of a user story.
• The tool should display changes in a side-by-side or inline format for easy
comparison.
• Users must be able to select any two versions from the version history for
comparison.
• Additions, deletions, and modifications between versions should be highlighted.
2.2.6 Security and Authentication
1. The module shall provide functionality for user authentication and login, ensuring
that only authorized personnel can access the system.
Details:
• The system must require users to provide valid credentials (username and
password) for login.
• The module should support multi-factor authentication (MFA) to enhance se-
curity.
26
2. Project Requirements
• Users must be notified of successful login or authentication failure with ap-
propriate error messages.
• The system should log all login attempts, including successful and failed at-
tempts, for auditing purposes.
2. The module shall implement role-based access control (RBAC) to manage user per-
missions and ensure that users only have access to the resources they are authorized
to access.
Details:
• The system must allow administrators to define and manage user roles.
• Each role should have specific permissions associated with it, controlling ac-
cess to different parts of the system.
• Users should be assigned roles based on their responsibilities and level of
access required.
• The system must enforce role-based permissions at all times, preventing unau-
thorized access.
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
The non-functional requirements for this system define the critical quality attributes that
will ensure the system’s reliability, performance, and overall user experience. These re-
quirements are measurable, ensuring the system meets specific performance benchmarks,
scalability expectations, security standards, and user accessibility needs.
2.3.1 Performance
The system must deliver rapid processing to facilitate real-time usage. From processing
the input document and prompt to generating the users stories and then scoring generated
user stories, the entire process must be fast and precise. An estimated time of 10-15 sec-
onds is desirable. The system allow simultaneous users activities without any observable
delay or significant performance deterioration. This performance metric ensures that the
system remains efficient, even during periods of high demand.
2.3.2 Scalability
As the system is expected to handle growing user bases over time, it must be scalable. The
system should support multiple concurrent users, adapt to increased workload without
27
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
performance bottlenecks. This scalability should be achieved through horizontal scaling
mechanisms, such as adding more servers or cloud-based resources when necessary.The
system architecture must be structured to accommodate an increased volume of traffic
and/or document size, maintaining optimal performance.
2.3.3 Security
Data security is paramount for this system, given that it handles sensitive project docu-
mentation and proprietary information. All stored data, including SRS documents and
generated user stories, must be encrypted using AES-256 encryption, ensuring maximum
data protection. In addition, the system must implement secure user authentication meth-
ods, following OAuth 2.0 standards to protect user accounts and access controls. Role-
based access control (RBAC) will further ensure that different types of users (e.g., admins,
developers, and reviewers) have appropriate levels of access, preventing unauthorized data
manipulation.
2.3.4 Usability
For the system to be widely used/accepted, it must prioritize user friendliness. The user
interface will be designed to be intuitive and user-friendly, ensuring users can easily up-
load SRS documents and prompts, even without technical knowledge. Compliance with
WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards will be mandatory. Error handling within the system
must be comprehensive, providing users with clear, actionable feedback to correct input
mistakes without external support, thereby ensuring a seamless user experience.
2.3.5 Relibility
The system is expected to function continuously with minimal interruptions. To this end,
it will maintain a service uptime of 90%, ensuring availability for users around the clock.
In the event of a system failure, automatic failover mechanisms must be in place to restore
services within 1-10 minutes of downtime. Additionally, daily backups of user data to
safeguard against data loss or corruption will be implemented, ensuring the ability to
restore the system to a working state without significant data loss.
2.3.6 Maintainability
The system’s architecture must be designed for easy maintenance and future updates. The
codebase should adhere to SOLID design principles, promoting modularity and ease of
28
2. Project Requirements
change. Allowing developers to make adjustments or implement upgrades without sig-
nificant rewrites. Scheduled updates and patches must be deploy-able with minimal dis-
ruption, keeping system downtime to less than 1 hour. Moreover, comprehensive system
logs will track system performance and errors, ensuring that maintenance tasks, such as
troubleshooting and diagnostics, can be completed efficiently.

3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.1 Box and Line Diagram
Figure 3.1: Box and Line diagram for complete architecture

3. System Overview
Box and line diagram in Figure 3.13.1 represent how different modules interact with each
other to achieve the system’s overall functionality. Security and Authentication module is
responsible for authentication of each user. Once verified, users can interact with appli-
cation via user input module where they submit SRS document & prompts. These inputs
are stored in database for further processing.
The SRS document analysis step involves extracting necessary details from document
such as functional and non-functional requirements with the help of NLP Engine module.
This process is crucial, as extracted requirements act as primary input for generation of
user stories along with textual prompt given by user.
Once user stories are generated, they go through Story Review and Editing module where
user can view and make modifications according to his/her needs.This module if also con-
nected with version history which allows users to track changes in user stories. All edited
user stories are stored in Database.
To manage different versions of user stories, the system uses Version Control and Track-
ing Module. This module makes sure that each iteration of user stories are recorded for
maintenance of history of changes in chronological order. The architecture ensures that
user stories are not only generated efficiently but also stored securely with robust tracking
system.
32
3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.2 Model, View and Controller (MVC) Diagram
Figure 3.2: MVC diagram for complete architecture
The MVC architecture divides the SprintCraft application into three layers: Model, View,
and Controller. The Model manages data, such as users, SRS, and user stories, along
with version control. The View handles user interaction interfaces, including story gen-
eration, editing, and version history. The Controller acts as a bridge, processing inputs
and updating the Model and Views accordingly. Summary for SprintCraft: The MVC
architecture for SprintCraft organizes the app into three components. The Model man-
ages data and logic, handling user stories, SRS data, version control, and authentication.
The View presents user interfaces for input, story generation, editing, and version his-
tory. The Controller processes user actions, updating both the Model and View to ensure
smooth user story generation and management. This architecture simplifies maintenance
and scalability.

ate formatting etc. System will also validate if content in file is valid or not. Application
will try to extract Functional and Non-functional requirements.

NLP Engine, as shown in Figure 3.53.5 and Figure 3.63.6 follow a structured workflow that

skims through whole document in order to gather functional and non-functional require-
ments. During this process, it also tags components as goal, constraints etc which are
crucial for understanding. If document contains technical jargon, the system attempts to
interpret its meaning and decode its complexity.
Once analysis is complete, it will receive user prompt. If the prompt contains sufficient

user will be notified about this ambiguity.

formation is insufficient in prompt, user is once again notify until both conditions are met.
This unified description shows how the NLP engine facilitates requirement extraction and
user story generation while providing feedback loops for handling errors or ambiguities
at different stages.

3.2.6 Module 6: Security and Authentication Module
Figure 3.13: Module 6: Activity Diagram
44
3.2 Design Models
Figure 3.14: Module 6: System Sequence Diagram
Figure 3.133.13 and Figure 3.143.14 represent flow of this module. It handles user login procedure
and role-based access control (RBAC). When user opens login page of application, he/she
is asked to enter their credentials. Once submitted, the system check if credential are valid
by cross checking it with database. If invalid, user is asked to enter id and password again
and makes sure that user is unable to access any sensitive information.
If credentials are valid, system proceeds to perform Multi Factor Authentication(MFA).
Outcome of this step will decide if user should be allowed to access information or not.
Authentication code is sent via email to user and system will be waiting for user to enter
authentication code. If code entered is invalid, then he will not be allowed to access data
and if code is valid then user will be allowed to access application.
Upon successful login, system check user roles and grants access to resources based on
their role through RBAC. This will make sure that any user can not access data that is not
45
3. System Overview
meant for him. The system then logs all login attempts for auditing purposes, creating
a complete record of both successful and unsuccessful login attempts which will allow
system to check for pattern that might indicate if system is being hacked or not.
The overall design aims to enhance security by integrating multiple layers of validation,
auditing, and role-based access, ensuring proper control over resources that are being
accessed by user.

2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.1 User Input
1. The module shall provide functionality to allow users to upload SRS (Software Re-
quirements Specification) documents.
Details:
• The module should support document formats such as .doc, .docx, and .pdf.
• Users should be able to browse their local file system and select the SRS doc-
ument to upload.
• The system must notify users upon successful upload or in case of failure with
appropriate error messages.
2. The module shall provide a prompt input field where users can specify the type of
user stories they want to generate.
Details:
• The prompt field should support text input, allowing users to specify require-
ments for functional, non-functional, or specific sections of the SRS.
• The interface should guide the users with examples or suggestions on how to
write effective prompts.
22
2. Project Requirements
• The system must process and store the prompt for subsequent user story gen-
eration.
3. The module shall validate the uploaded SRS document and the prompt input to en-
sure they meet the required format and structure.
Details:
• The system must check the file type of the uploaded document and reject
unsupported formats with an appropriate error message.
• The prompt field must have validation rules to ensure the prompt meets the
minimum and maximum length requirements.
• The system should provide real-time feedback to users if the uploaded docu-
ment or entered prompt does not meet the required criteria.
• The validation process must be efficient to ensure a smooth user experience.
2.2.2 Natural Language Processing (NLP) Engine
1. The NLP engine shall extract key requirements from the uploaded SRS document.
Details:
• The engine should identify and extract functional and non-functional require-
ments from the SRS document.
• It should recognize and tag different components such as goals, constraints,
and assumptions.
• The engine must handle diverse document structures and terminologies to ac-
curately capture the requirements.
2. The NLP engine shall understand the context of the SRS document to accurately
interpret its content.
Details:
• The engine must be capable of handling complex language constructs and
technical jargon to derive meaningful insights.
3. The NLP engine shall interpret the user’s prompt to generate tailored user stories.
Details:
23
2.2 Functional Requirements
• The engine should process the user’s prompt to understand specific require-
ments for user stories (e.g., focus on particular sections, functional vs. non-
functional).
• It must translate technical language and detailed requirements into clear, user-
friendly language suitable for user stories.
• The generated user stories should be coherent, contextually accurate, and
aligned with the user’s prompt specifications.
• The engine should provide feedback to users if the prompt is ambiguous or
insufficient for generating meaningful user stories.
2.2.3 User Story Generation
1. The module shall automatically generate user stories from the data extracted by the
NLP engine.
Details:
• The system should adhere to a predefined format for user stories, such as “As
a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]”.
• The module must ensure that each user story is coherent, concise, and follows
the standard user story structure.
• The generation process should be efficient, providing quick results to the user.
2. The module shall be capable of generating user stories for both functional and non-
functional requirements.
Details:
• The system should distinguish between functional and non-functional require-
ments and generate appropriate user stories for each.
• Functional requirements user stories should focus on describing the actions
and features of the system.
• Non-functional requirements user stories should address performance, secu-
rity, usability, and other quality attributes.
• The module must ensure that all relevant requirements extracted by the NLP
engine are covered in the generated user stories.
3. The module shall allow customization of user stories based on the user’s prompt.
Details:
24
2. Project Requirements
• The module should interpret the user’s prompt to tailor the user stories accord-
ing to the specific instructions provided.
• It should allow customization in terms of focus areas, level of detail, and spe-
cific sections or types of requirements.
• The system must ensure that the generated user stories align with the user’s
prompts while maintaining coherence and clarity.
2.2.4 Story Review and Editing
1. The module shall allow users to review and edit the generated user stories.
Details:
• Users should be able to modify the text of the user stories to better fit their
needs or correct any inaccuracies.
• The interface should provide a user-friendly text editor with basic formatting
options (e.g., bold, italic, underline).
• Changes made by users should be saved and reflected in the final user story
output.
• The system must provide version control to track changes and allow users to
revert to previous versions if needed.
2. The module shall provide mechanisms for users to give feedback on the generated
user stories and suggest adjustments to the generation rules.
Details:
• Users should be able to submit feedback on specific user stories, including
suggestions for improvements or noting any issues.
• The system should collect and analyze feedback to identify common patterns
or areas for improvement in the story generation process.
• The interface should allow users to propose new rules or modifications to ex-
isting rules for generating user stories.
• The module should facilitate communication between users and developers to
ensure feedback is addressed and incorporated into future updates.
25
2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.5 V ersion Control and Tracking
1. The module shall provide functionality to track all changes made to user stories,
capturing each version as a separate entity.
Details:
• The system should create a new version of a user story every time it is edited
and saved.
• Metadata for each version, including the author of the changes, timestamp,
and a brief description of the changes, must be stored.
• A version history view, listing all versions of a user story in chronological
order, should be available.
• Users should have the ability to revert to any previous version of a user story.
2. The module shall provide functionality for users to compare different versions of a
user story to identify changes.
Details:
• A comparison tool should be available to highlight differences between two
selected versions of a user story.
• The tool should display changes in a side-by-side or inline format for easy
comparison.
• Users must be able to select any two versions from the version history for
comparison.
• Additions, deletions, and modifications between versions should be highlighted.
2.2.6 Security and Authentication
1. The module shall provide functionality for user authentication and login, ensuring
that only authorized personnel can access the system.
Details:
• The system must require users to provide valid credentials (username and
password) for login.
• The module should support multi-factor authentication (MFA) to enhance se-
curity.
26
2. Project Requirements
• Users must be notified of successful login or authentication failure with ap-
propriate error messages.
• The system should log all login attempts, including successful and failed at-
tempts, for auditing purposes.
2. The module shall implement role-based access control (RBAC) to manage user per-
missions and ensure that users only have access to the resources they are authorized
to access.
Details:
• The system must allow administrators to define and manage user roles.
• Each role should have specific permissions associated with it, controlling ac-
cess to different parts of the system.
• Users should be assigned roles based on their responsibilities and level of
access required.
• The system must enforce role-based permissions at all times, preventing unau-
thorized access.
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
The non-functional requirements for this system define the critical quality attributes that
will ensure the system’s reliability, performance, and overall user experience. These re-
quirements are measurable, ensuring the system meets specific performance benchmarks,
scalability expectations, security standards, and user accessibility needs.
2.3.1 Performance
The system must deliver rapid processing to facilitate real-time usage. From processing
the input document and prompt to generating the users stories and then scoring generated
user stories, the entire process must be fast and precise. An estimated time of 10-15 sec-
onds is desirable. The system allow simultaneous users activities without any observable
delay or significant performance deterioration. This performance metric ensures that the
system remains efficient, even during periods of high demand.
2.3.2 Scalability
As the system is expected to handle growing user bases over time, it must be scalable. The
system should support multiple concurrent users, adapt to increased workload without
27
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
performance bottlenecks. This scalability should be achieved through horizontal scaling
mechanisms, such as adding more servers or cloud-based resources when necessary.The
system architecture must be structured to accommodate an increased volume of traffic
and/or document size, maintaining optimal performance.
2.3.3 Security
Data security is paramount for this system, given that it handles sensitive project docu-
mentation and proprietary information. All stored data, including SRS documents and
generated user stories, must be encrypted using AES-256 encryption, ensuring maximum
data protection. In addition, the system must implement secure user authentication meth-
ods, following OAuth 2.0 standards to protect user accounts and access controls. Role-
based access control (RBAC) will further ensure that different types of users (e.g., admins,
developers, and reviewers) have appropriate levels of access, preventing unauthorized data
manipulation.
2.3.4 Usability
For the system to be widely used/accepted, it must prioritize user friendliness. The user
interface will be designed to be intuitive and user-friendly, ensuring users can easily up-
load SRS documents and prompts, even without technical knowledge. Compliance with
WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards will be mandatory. Error handling within the system
must be comprehensive, providing users with clear, actionable feedback to correct input
mistakes without external support, thereby ensuring a seamless user experience.
2.3.5 Relibility
The system is expected to function continuously with minimal interruptions. To this end,
it will maintain a service uptime of 90%, ensuring availability for users around the clock.
In the event of a system failure, automatic failover mechanisms must be in place to restore
services within 1-10 minutes of downtime. Additionally, daily backups of user data to
safeguard against data loss or corruption will be implemented, ensuring the ability to
restore the system to a working state without significant data loss.
2.3.6 Maintainability
The system’s architecture must be designed for easy maintenance and future updates. The
codebase should adhere to SOLID design principles, promoting modularity and ease of
28
2. Project Requirements
change. Allowing developers to make adjustments or implement upgrades without sig-
nificant rewrites. Scheduled updates and patches must be deploy-able with minimal dis-
ruption, keeping system downtime to less than 1 hour. Moreover, comprehensive system
logs will track system performance and errors, ensuring that maintenance tasks, such as
troubleshooting and diagnostics, can be completed efficiently.

3. System Overview
Box and line diagram in Figure 3.13.1 represent how different modules interact with each
other to achieve the system’s overall functionality. Security and Authentication module is
responsible for authentication of each user. Once verified, users can interact with appli-
cation via user input module where they submit SRS document & prompts. These inputs
are stored in database for further processing.
The SRS document analysis step involves extracting necessary details from document
such as functional and non-functional requirements with the help of NLP Engine module.
This process is crucial, as extracted requirements act as primary input for generation of
user stories along with textual prompt given by user.
Once user stories are generated, they go through Story Review and Editing module where
user can view and make modifications according to his/her needs.This module if also con-
nected with version history which allows users to track changes in user stories. All edited
user stories are stored in Database.
To manage different versions of user stories, the system uses Version Control and Track-
ing Module. This module makes sure that each iteration of user stories are recorded for
maintenance of history of changes in chronological order. The architecture ensures that
user stories are not only generated efficiently but also stored securely with robust tracking
system.
32
3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.2 Model, View and Controller (MVC) Diagram
Figure 3.2: MVC diagram for complete architecture
The MVC architecture divides the SprintCraft application into three layers: Model, View,
and Controller. The Model manages data, such as users, SRS, and user stories, along
with version control. The View handles user interaction interfaces, including story gen-
eration, editing, and version history. The Controller acts as a bridge, processing inputs
and updating the Model and Views accordingly. Summary for SprintCraft: The MVC
architecture for SprintCraft organizes the app into three components. The Model man-
ages data and logic, handling user stories, SRS data, version control, and authentication.
The View presents user interfaces for input, story generation, editing, and version his-
tory. The Controller processes user actions, updating both the Model and View to ensure
smooth user story generation and management. This architecture simplifies maintenance
and scalability.

auditing, and role-based access, ensuring proper control over resources that are being
accessed by user.

IntroductionThe purpose of this mid evaluation report is to define the scope, systemlevel requirements, andfirstiteration deliverables for our VRbasedapplicationnamed“FearLESS”. FearLESS helps peopleovercometheirfearsofheights(Acrophobia)anddarkness(Nyctophobia)usingVR, gaugesaperson’sfearintensity,and connects its users to therapists/psychiatrists for counseling. This document is intended for theFearLESS team, our supervisor Mr. Bilal Khalid Dar, our co-supervisor Mr. Wasif Ali Wasif, andtheFYPpanel.
ProblemStatementFear is a natural survival response to danger that becomes irrational, dysfunctional, and even phobicwhen the level of danger is insignificant compared to the reaction it caused [1]. Such irrational fearsand/or phobias vary in intensity amongst individuals. They oftendisturbthesuffering individual’s dailylife, making simple daily activities and pleasures difficult to achieve, as they go out of their way toovercometheirfear.
For instance, anirrational fear of heightswouldinvolvethepersonhavingpanicattacksbystandingonaladder or walking up a flight of stairs. A person’s fear of darkness may cause them to miss sleep,depression, and they limit their life to the daytime since leaving the house after dark becomes anextremely stressful and difficult task. Avoidance strategies do help the person avoid their fears andtriggers, but it canalsolimit what a personcandointheirlife, andmayevenbecomedangerousfortheperson. An irrational fear of clowns may cause the person to miss their family’s birthday parties andcelebrations. Likewise, a person can miss medical appointments, and life saving drugs due to theirintensefearofneedles.
According totheNational Comorbidity Survey, among theadults withphobias intheUnitedStates, “anestimated 21.9% had high fear intensity, 30.0% had moderate impairment, and 48.1% had mildimpairment” [3]. Treatment for such fears, and phobias include exposure therapy andcognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), however, only about23%ofthepeoplewithphobiasseektreatment[2]. Taking therapyorevencheckingtheintensityofone’sfearrequiresfundsandaccesstoprofessionalswhich most people end up avoiding, either because of their geographic location, or social stigma thatcomes with seeking help frommental health professionals. Moreover, it is a challenge tocontrol andreplicatetheexposuretherapytreatmentinasafeenvironment.
ProblemSolutionThe proposed solution to the above problemstatement is FearLESS, an application that uses VirtualReality ExposureTherapy (VRET)tohelpusersconfirmandovercometheirirrational fears/phobias. WiththehelpofFearLESS, userscanexposethemselvestotheirfears, usingsimulations, atvaryingintensities,until theycanconquerandcontrol theirreactionstotheirirrational fears/phobias.
To confirmthe presence of irrational fear/phobia, and to determine theseverity of thefear, FearLESSallows users to take a questionnaire before they can view the simulations. The results of the
4
questionnaireareusedby FearLESS toeither adjust theintensity ofthefearshowninthesimulation, orto suggest psychiatrists and therapists, availableonFearLESS, toguide, andhelptheusers beforetheyviewthe simulations. This is done for the user’s mental safety. Furthermore, users canalsousethesequestionnairestotracktheirprogress, astheyfacetheirfearsinthesimulations.
Thefears that areofferedby theapplicationincludeAcrophobia(fear of heights) [4], andNyctophobia(fear of darkness) [8]. Thesimulations for thesefears areprovidedtotheusers inthreedifferentlevels(increasing intensity). Thelevel users canview(as many times they need)isbasedonthequestionnairescore. Therapists canaccess thesimulations toprovideexposuretherapyfromthecomfortoftheirownoffice/clinic.
ModulesOurprojectincludes8differentmodules, whichareasfollows:1. PhobiaSelection:This module allows users to view exposure therapy simulations for different fears/phobias.FearLESSwill offersimulationsfortwofears, namelyAcrophobiaandNyctophobia.
2. SimulationLevels:This moduleislinkedtothePhobiaSimulationmodule. Foreachfear, userswill beprovidedwiththreesimulations, namelyeasy, mediumandhardlevel, torepresentincreasingfearintensity.
3. Professional GuidanceThis moduleallows users torequest and/orbeassignedcounselingfromtherapists/psychiatristsfor their fears. Therapists can view users' questionnaire results, and can communicate withassignedusersusingtextmessaging, andvideochat.
4. IntensityConfirmationThis module allows users to take a questionnairetoconfirmthepresenceof their fear, andtodeterminetheintensitylevel oftheirfear.
5. ProgressTrackingThis moduleallows userstodeterminetheirprogressforexposuretherapyusingquestionnaires.This module is linked to the User Profile Management Module, as only users whohavemadetheirprofilescanaccessthismodule.
6. PaymentModuleThismoduleprovidestherapists/psychiatristswiththeabilitytosetuptheirpaymentdetails, anditletsusersthatneedcounselingwiththeirexposuretherapytopayforthesessions.
7. UserProfileManagement
5
This module provides Login, andSignupfacilities along withprofilepagemanagement tobothusers, andtherapists.
8. FeedbackThis module allows users to give and viewfeedback for thesimulations availablefor exposuretherapy, andratethetherapist/psychiatristthatisassignedtotheuserforexposuretherapy.

TypeofUser Description
Acrophobic(Heights)Users Acrophobic people are those that have been diagnosed withacrophobia (fear of heights), and will use FearLESS toget exposuretherapy for fear of heights. Such users, with the help of thesimulations, will expose themselves to increasing heights until theyhaveeither overcometheirfear, orfeel thesymptomsoftheirphobiatoalesserextent. TheycanalsouseFearLESStogetonlinecounselingfromtheapplication’scounselingmodule.
Nyctophobic(Darkness)Users Nyctophobic people are those that have been diagnosed withnyctophobia (fear ofdarkness), andwill useFearLESStogetexposuretherapy for fear of darkness. Such users, with the help of thesimulations, will exposethemselves toincreasing darkness until theyhaveeither overcometheirfear, orfeel thesymptomsoftheirphobiatoalesserextent. TheycanalsouseFearLESStogetonlinecounselingfromtheapplication’scounselingmodule.
InquisitiveUsers These are users that are curious about whether or not they haveafear of heights and/or darkness. Such users will rely on the resultsgiventothembytheapplication’squestionnaires, andtheirreactionstothesimulationsbeforetheyconfirmiftheyhavethefearornot.
Counselors(Therapists/Psychiatrists)
Therapists, psychiatrists or counseling professionals that areproviding exposuretherapy topeoplecanuseFearLESS’ssimulationsto provide their patients exposure therapy fromthe safety of theiroffices. They can also use the questionnaires to determine theirpatient’sfearintensitytowardsheightsand/ordarkness.
VREnthusiasts Suchuserswill useFearLESSonlytoattaintheVRexperience.

SystemOverviewThe following section contains design documentation and diagrams that best illustrate FearLESS’sfunctionality. The architectural design diagram, and data flows represents the architecture, and datasinks/sources of the entire system respectively. Meanwhile the design models including activitydiagrams, system level sequence diagrams, and state transition diagrams represent the profilemanagementmoduleoftheFearLESSapplication, asitwaspartofthefirstiterationdeliverables.
SystemsArchitectural DesignThe architecture pattern that best describes our applicationFearLESS is microservices, as showninthediagrambelow, andthisisforseveral reasons. Firstly, FearLESSiscomposedofseveral modules/features,such as the therapist services, payment and simulation services, that can be developed andimplemented independently. Secondly, microservices gives us an opportunity toaddnewservices andscale existing services without affecting the current functionality and integrity of the application. Forexample, having a VR service, in the Player Session Microservice, gives us the flexibility to add morelevels or simulations for other phobias intoourapplication. Finally, FearLESSisalsointegratedwiththirdparty APIs suchasTwilo(forreal-timevideochattingandtextmessaging), andPlayFab(fordatastorage),whichcanbeeasilyintegrated, duetothemicroservicearchitecture.
Figure3: SystemArchitectureDiagram
26

# FYP1-MidReport-F24-029-D-VerseCraft - i210438 Muhammad Waleed

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
pabilities. The main functionalities include chapter management, modifiable outline bars,
notes creation, version control, and options to write in both English and Urdu. The ap-
plication will provide features like character and plot development windows, AI-powered
grammar checking, and writing suggestions. It will also support publishing options with
privacy controls. The boundaries of this project include focusing solely on providing an
end-to-end solution for writers, without delving into other unrelated creative activities
such as graphic design or video production. The solution will cater to both novice and
experienced writers who require robust tools to manage their writing projects efficiently.
1.3 Modules
The proposed project consists of several modules designed to provide key functionalities
to the users. Each module is outlined below:
1.3.1 Module 1: Workspace Dashboard
The workspace dashboard provides structured writing tools to help users organize and
streamline their workflow.
1. Chapter Organization and Outline Management
2. Collaborative Writing
3. Content Customization and Personalization
4. Notes Creation
5. Version Control
6. Language Options (English/Urdu)
7. Character Development Window
8. Plot Development Window
9. Publishing Options
1.3.2 Module 2: AI-Analysis Tools
This module provides AI-assisted features to improve the writing process.
1. Fine-tuned AI-Assistant for Writing Suggestions
2. English Grammar Checker
2
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
The following user classes are expected to interact with the VerseCraft application:
User Class Description
Writers Writers using the platform to create and manage their stories and novels.
They will use the workspace dashboard, AI tools, and collaboration features
to streamline their writing process.
Administrators Personnel responsible for managing the platform’s operation, ensuring sys-
tem security, managing user accounts, and offering support when needed.
Developers The technical team responsible for developing and maintaining the platform,
fixing bugs, integrating new features, and ensuring the platform’s function-
ality.
Testers Individuals responsible for testing the application for bugs, usability issues,
and ensuring the platform works as intended across different devices and
environments.
Documentation
Writers
Professionals tasked with creating user guides, tutorials, and other instruc-
tional content to help users and developers navigate the platform effectively.
Table 1.1: User Classes and Characteristics
3

Chapter 2
Project Requirements
This chapter describes the functional and non-functional requirements of the project.
2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
The selection of the requirement gathering technique(s) will depend on the type of project.
For instance, the following use cases outline the key interactions:
1. User Registration and Login
Allows users to create an account, log in with their credentials, or reset their pass-
word.
2. Create and View Writing Projects
Writers can create new writing projects and view existing projects.
3. Manage Chapters
Users can create, edit, delete, and rearrange chapters within a project.
4. Manage Notes
Writers can add, edit, and delete notes for specific chapters or the entire project.
5. Collaborative Writing
Multiple users can collaborate on the same project with role-based access and real-
time editing.
6. View and Comment on Published Projects
Readers can view published projects and leave comments or feedback.
7. Manage Account Settings
Users can update their profile, change email or password, and customize their pref-
erences.
5
2. Project Requirements
8. AI-Assisted Grammar Check
Writers can run grammar and spell checks on their projects using an AI-powered
assistant.
9. Manage Characters
Writers can create, edit, and track character profiles within a project.
10. Switch Language (English/Urdu)
Users can toggle between English and Urdu for both the interface and content cre-
ation.
11. Customize Workspace
Writers can change the appearance of their workspace and adjust settings to opti-
mize their writing environment.
12. Export Project (PDF, DOCX, etc.)
Allows users to export their entire project in various formats such as PDF or DOCX.
13. Version Control
Users can track changes, view the history of edits, and restore previous versions of
their project.
14. AI-Assisted Writing Assistant (ChatBot)
Users can interact with an AI chatbot to get suggestions, guidance, or answers to
questions during the writing process.
15. Manage Project (Edit, Delete)
Writers can edit the details of their project or delete it if necessary.
16. View Analytics and Writing Metrics
Provides writers with analytics on word count, session duration, chapter progres-
sion, and more.
17. Publish Completed Project
Allow writers to share their completed work with readers, publishers, or other . so
others can view it, like, or comment on it.
18. Manage Users and Projects (Admin)
Allow Admin to manage users and projects to maintain system order and address
user issues.
6
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
Figure 2.1: Use Case Diagram for VerseCraft
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
2.2.1 Use Case 1: User Registration and Login
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: User
Stakeholders and Interests
• Users: Need a secure and straightforward way to create and access their accounts.
• System Administrators: Ensure the security and integrity of user data.
• Developers and Designers: Focus on providing a seamless and user-friendly au-
thentication experience.
7
2. Project Requirements
Preconditions
• User must have access to the internet.
• User must provide a valid email address.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Users are successfully registered and can access their accounts.
• Users can securely reset their passwords if forgotten.
Main Success Scenario
1. User navigates to the registration page.
2. User enters required information (e.g., name, email, password).
3. System validates the input and creates a new user account.
4. System sends a confirmation email to the user.
5. User verifies their email and logs into their account.
Extensions
• 3a. If the email is already in use:
1. System notifies the user and prompts for a different email.
• 4a. If the user forgets their password:
1. User selects the ’Forgot Password’ option.
2. System sends a password reset link to the registered email.
3. User resets the password using the link.
Special Requirements
• Ensure compliance with data protection regulations.
• Implement CAPTCHA to prevent automated registrations.
8
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
Technology and Data Variations List
• Support for social media logins (e.g., Google, Facebook).
• Multi-factor authentication for enhanced security.
Frequency of Occurrence
• High, as all new users must register and existing users must log in regularly.
2.2.2 Use Case 2: Create and Manage Writing Projects
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Writer
Stakeholders and Interests
• Writers: Interested in an efficient way to create, manage, and track their writing
projects.
• System Administrators: Need to ensure that the system supports efficient data
handling and user management.
• Developers and Designers: Focus on providing an intuitive and feature-rich envi-
ronment for managing writing projects.
Preconditions
• User must be registered and logged in.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• New writing projects are created and saved correctly.
Main Success Scenario
1. Writer logs into their account.
2. Writer navigates to the project creation interface.
9
2. Project Requirements
3. Writer selects the option to create a new project.
4. Writer inputs details for the new project, such as title, description, genre, and initial
content.
5. System saves the new project and confirms its creation to the writer.
Extensions
• 3a. If the writer selects an existing project:
1. System displays project details.
• 4a. If input data is invalid:
1. System prompts for correct data.
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• Project data can be entered via web forms or direct text entry.
• Projects can be tagged with metadata such as genre, which may affect how they are
handled or displayed by the system.
Frequency of Occurrence
• Frequent, as writers will regularly manage their writing projects.
2.2.3 Use Case 3: Manage Chapters
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Writer
10
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
Stakeholders and Interests
• Writers: Interested in flexible and intuitive tools to structure their stories via chap-
ter management.
• Editors: Seek streamlined processes for revising and organizing content.
• System Administrators: Ensure robust functionality and user-friendly interfaces
for managing content.
Preconditions
• User must be registered, logged in, and must have an existing project.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Chapters within a project are correctly created, modified, deleted, or rearranged.
• Changes are saved and accurately reflected in the project.
Main Success Scenario
1. Writer logs into their account and opens an existing project.
2. Writer accesses the chapter management interface within the project.
3. Writer selects to add a new chapter, providing a title and initial content.
4. System adds the chapter to the project at the specified location.
5. Writer edits the content of any chapter or rearranges chapters as needed.
6. System saves changes and updates the project layout.
7. Writer can delete any chapter, with the system confirming deletion and updating the
project accordingly.
Extensions
• 3a. If the writer edits an existing chapter:
1. System loads the chapter’s content for editing.
2. Writer modifies content and saves changes.
11
2. Project Requirements
• 5a. If input data during editing is invalid:
1. System prompts to correct the data.
• 6a. If the writer wants to rearrange chapters:
1. System allows drag-and-drop functionality to reorder chapters.
2. System confirms the new order.
• 7a. If the writer deletes a chapter:
1. System asks for confirmation before deletion.
2. Upon confirmation, the chapter is deleted and remaining chapters are adjusted.
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• Chapters can be edited through a text editor interface that supports rich text format-
ting.
• Rearranging chapters utilizes a drag-and-drop interface.
Frequency of Occurrence
• Regular, as managing chapters is a fundamental part of structuring a writing project.
2.2.4 Use Case 4: Manage Notes (Inline Notes)
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Writer
Stakeholders and Interests
• Writers: Need efficient ways to annotate their manuscripts for better organization
and referencing.
• Editors: Benefit from clear, accessible notes that enhance the editing process.
12
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
• System Administrators: Ensure the notes feature is seamless and integrated effec-
tively with the rest of the writing environment.
Preconditions
• User must be logged in and have an existing project with at least one chapter.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Notes are accurately created, modified, or deleted within the project.
• Changes to notes are saved and can be referenced or removed as needed.
Main Success Scenario
1. Writer accesses an existing project and selects a chapter.
2. Writer chooses to add a new note linked to a specific part of the chapter text.
3. Writer enters the note content and saves it, which is then displayed inline or as a
margin note.
4. Writer edits existing notes by selecting them and modifying the content.
5. System saves the updated notes.
6. Writer can delete any note, with the system confirming the deletion and removing
the note.
Extensions
• 3a. If the writer inputs an excessively long note:
1. System prompts to shorten the note to meet character limits.
• 4a. If the writer attempts to save an empty note:
1. System prompts to add content before saving.
• 6a. If the writer deletes a note:
1. System asks for confirmation to prevent accidental deletions.
13
2. Project Requirements
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• Notes are text-based but may support basic formatting to distinguish them from the
main text.
• The interface for notes might offer options for color coding or tagging for better
organization.
Frequency of Occurrence
• Frequent, as notes are a critical part of the writing and editing process.
2.2.5 Use Case 5: Collaborative Writing
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Collaborative Writers
Stakeholders and Interests
• Collaborative Writers: Interested in a seamless and synchronized writing experi-
ence with other authors.
• Project Manager: Oversees the collaborative process, ensuring all participants can
contribute effectively.
• System Administrators: Ensure the system supports multiple users simultane-
ously without performance degradation.
Preconditions
• All users must be registered and have permissions set for the project.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Multiple users can access the project simultaneously.
14
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
• Changes by one user are visible in real-time to all others.
• Role-based permissions are respected and enforced.
Main Success Scenario
1. Project creator invites collaborators to the project and assigns roles.
2. Collaborators log into the system and access the shared project.
3. Users make real-time edits to the project, with changes instantly visible to all par-
ticipants.
4. System continuously saves the project state, ensuring no data loss.
5. Collaborators use communication tools integrated within the platform to discuss
and coordinate efforts.
6. Project manager reviews roles and access permissions periodically to ensure proper
collaboration flow.
Extensions
• 1a. If an invitee does not have an account:
1. Invitee is prompted to register before accessing the project.
• 3a. If conflicts arise from simultaneous edits:
1. System flags the conflict and prompts users to resolve it.
• 5a. If a user encounters system performance issues:
1. System admin is notified to troubleshoot and optimize performance.
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• Edits are made through a web-based text editor capable of handling simultaneous
inputs.
• Role-based access control system to manage permissions for viewing, editing, and
administrative actions.
15
2. Project Requirements
Frequency of Occurrence
• Regular, especially in projects involving multiple authors or large-scale collabora-
tive efforts.

Primary Actor: Reader

6. Other readers and the author can view and respond to the comment.

2. User selects either English or Urdu from the language options.
